items.evidence_text:
Client: Thanks. Honestly, I just want a clear plan. Post-divorce I’m trying to stabilize cash flow and not make a dumb mistake with investing.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start high-level and then we’ll drill down. What’s your gross income looking like across everything right now—salary, any rental income, all of it?
Client: All-in, gross for the year is 212150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, you mentioned rentals—do you have more than one rental income stream or is it just one?
Client: It’s kind of split. There’s rental income that comes in monthly, and then another chunk that’s annual—like a once-a-year distribution.

Client: It’s kind of split. There’s rental income that comes in monthly, and then another chunk that’s annual—like a once-a-year distribution.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Switching to outflows for a second—about what are your total monthly expenses, all-in? Mortgage, utilities, food, insurance, everything.
Client: Monthly expenses total is 15050.
Advisor: Thanks. One sec—just writing that down. Within that, how much is debt payments each month—like the mortgage payment or any other loans?
Client: It’s basically the mortgage. Monthly debt cost total is 4100.

Client: Monthly expenses total is 15050.
Advisor: Thanks. One sec—just writing that down. Within that, how much is debt payments each month—like the mortgage payment or any other loans?
Client: It’s basically the mortgage. Monthly debt cost total is 4100.
Advisor: Okay. And that mortgage—what’s the current balance and rate, roughly, and when does it end?
Client: Balance outstanding is 247200, the interest rate is 7.46%, and the final payment date is 2031-09-08.

Client: Balance outstanding is 247200, the interest rate is 7.46%, and the final payment date is 2031-09-08.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll come back to whether refinancing or accelerated payoff makes sense at that rate. Now, on the asset side—what do you have invested or set aside that you’d consider investable assets, excluding the home value?
Client: Investable assets total is 490800.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Can you walk me through what that’s made up of—like taxable accounts, retirement accounts, cash at a bank, anything on an advisor platform, that kind of thing?
Client: Sure. I’ve got a taxable account on a retirement platform—value is 232700.

Client: And I’ve got some private markets stuff on an advisor platform—80750.
Advisor: Great, that’s a clean breakdown. And then separate from investable assets, you do have your primary residence—what’s the current value you’re using for that?
Client: The primary residence value is 347250.
Advisor: Got it. Any life insurance, disability coverage, or anything like that we should factor in right now?
Client: No, nothing in place at the moment.

Client: It’s basically the mortgage. Monthly debt cost total is 4100.
Advisor: Okay. And that mortgage—what’s the current balance and rate, roughly, and when does it end?
Client: Balance outstanding is 247200, the interest rate is 7.46%, and the final payment date is 2031-09-08.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll come back to whether refinancing or accelerated payoff makes sense at that rate. Now, on the asset side—what do you have invested or set aside that you’d consider investable assets, excluding the home value?
Client: Investable assets total is 490800.

Client: Thanks. Honestly, I just want a clear plan. Post-divorce I’m trying to stabilize cash flow and not make a dumb mistake with investing.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start high-level and then we’ll drill down. What’s your gross income looking like across everything right now—salary, any rental income, all of it?
Client: All-in, gross for the year is 212150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, you mentioned rentals—do you have more than one rental income stream or is it just one?
Client: It’s kind of split. There’s rental income that comes in monthly, and then another chunk that’s annual—like a once-a-year distribution.

Client: All-in, gross for the year is 212150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, you mentioned rentals—do you have more than one rental income stream or is it just one?
Client: It’s kind of split. There’s rental income that comes in monthly, and then another chunk that’s annual—like a once-a-year distribution.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Switching to outflows for a second—about what are your total monthly expenses, all-in? Mortgage, utilities, food, insurance, everything.
Client: Monthly expenses total is 15050.

Advisor: Okay. Any dependents we should plan around—kids, anyone you financially support?
Client: No—0 dependents.
Advisor: Perfect. And then last admin-ish one: how are we engaging—are you working with me through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else like a brokerage-only setup?
Client: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. One sec, I’m just noting those down.

Advisor: While I’ve got you, Thomas, anything you want me to prioritize next meeting—cash-flow tightness versus the 7.46% mortgage versus investment allocation?
Client: Mortgage first, honestly. That 7.46% bugs me, and I want to understand refinance versus just paying it down faster.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll run both paths with your numbers—income, expenses, and that single mortgage—and keep it conservative so it doesn’t feel like a big swing.
Advisor: Okay—mortgage first next time. Before we hop off, Thomas, I want to lock in a couple planning assumptions so I don’t build the wrong model.
Advisor: On risk—when markets drop, do you see yourself as conservative, moderate, or aggressive?

Advisor: On risk—when markets drop, do you see yourself as conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client: Conservative. Like, I can’t stomach big drawdowns—so conservative.
Advisor: Got it. And just confirming your current marital status for the plan—are you divorced, married, single, widowed?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Okay. And country for tax and account assumptions—are we planning everything as US-based?

Advisor: Hi Thomas—good to meet you. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client: Thanks. Honestly, I just want a clear plan. Post-divorce I’m trying to stabilize cash flow and not make a dumb mistake with investing.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start high-level and then we’ll drill down. What’s your gross income looking like across everything right now—salary, any rental income, all of it?
Client: All-in, gross for the year is 212150.

Client: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Last one: roughly where do you land tax-bracket-wise—like 24, 32, 35, 37—what should I assume?
Client: I’m in the 35% bracket band.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. And just so I’m not mixing this up: that 35% is your bracket band assumption we should use for projections, not necessarily your effective rate.
Client: Right—exactly. Use 35% for the bracket band assumption.
